perturbation.original_text:
The Receiving Party undertakes during the term of this Agreement and for a period of five (5) years after the date of its expiry or termination:
- to safeguard Confidential Information as it does for its own proprietary information of like importance, but at least with a reasonable degree of care,
perturbation.changed_text:
The Receiving Party undertakes during the term of this Agreement and for a period of two (2) years after the date of its expiry or termination:
- to safeguard Confidential Information as it does for its own proprietary information of like importance, but at least with a reasonable degree of care,
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the confidentiality period to two years may be insufficient to protect certain types of trade secrets, potentially conflicting with state trade secret laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: Many states have adopted the Uniform Trade Secrets Act (UTSA). While the UTSA does not specify a fixed period for trade secret protection, reducing the contractual protection period to two years may be deemed insufficient to provide reasonable protection for information meeting the definition of a trade secret under the UTSA, especially if the information has a long commercial life and high value. The UTSA defines trade secrets as information that derives independent economic value from not being generally known and is subject to reasonable efforts to maintain its secrecy. A two-year protection period may not align with the required 'reasonable efforts'.

perturbation.justification: The change from a five-year to a two-year confidentiality period contradicts the principles of trade secret protection, as it may be deemed insufficient to provide reasonable protection for trade secrets, especially those with long commercial lives. While the provided snippets are not helpful, the Law Explanation indicates a potential conflict with the UTSA, which requires 'reasonable efforts' to maintain secrecy. A two-year protection period could be seen as failing to meet this standard, thus creating a legally significant contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Any dispute arising in connection with this Agreement, including any dispute related to the validity, interpretation, implementation and/or termination of the Agreement, which cannot be amicably settled within the sixty (60) days following its occurrence shall be finally settled under the Rules of Arbitration of the International Chamber of Commerce by one arbitrator appointed in accordance with those Rules.
perturbation.changed_text: Any dispute arising in connection with this Agreement, including any dispute related to the validity, interpretation, implementation and/or termination of the Agreement, which cannot be amicably settled within the thirty (30) days following its occurrence shall be finally settled under the Rules of Arbitration of the International Chamber of Commerce by one arbitrator appointed in accordance with those Rules.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the amicable settlement period to 30 days may not provide sufficient time for parties to resolve complex disputes, potentially conflicting with the principles of good faith negotiation and alternative dispute resolution encouraged by courts.
perturbation.law_explanation: While the FAA generally favors arbitration, it does not override fundamental contract law principles, including the requirement of good faith. A 30-day settlement period might be deemed unreasonably short in certain cases, particularly those involving complex technical or legal issues. This could be seen as undermining the good faith requirement implicit in contractual agreements and potentially rendering the arbitration agreement unenforceable if it effectively prevents meaningful pre-arbitration negotiation.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 9 U.S. Code § 2 - Validity, irrevocability, and enforcement of agreements to arbitrate | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 9 U.S. Code § 2 - Validity, irrevocability, and enforcement of agreements to arbitrate U.S. Code Notes prev | next A written provision in any maritime transaction or a contract evidencing a transaction involving commerce to settle by arbitration a controversy thereafter arising out of such contract or transaction, or the refusal to perform the whole or any part thereof, or an agreement in writing to submit to arbitration an existing controversy arising out of such a contract, transaction, or refusal, shall be valid, irrevocable, and enforceable, save upon such grounds as exist at law or in equity for the revocation of any contract or as otherwise provided in chapter 4. perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the amicable settlement period from 60 to 30 days. The FAA, as evidenced by 9 U.S. Code § 2, generally enforces arbitration agreements, but this enforcement is 'save upon such grounds as exist at law or in equity for the revocation of any contract'. The Law Explanation suggests that a 30-day settlement period might be deemed unreasonably short, potentially undermining the good faith requirement implicit in contractual agreements. This directly contradicts the intent to provide a reasonable opportunity for dispute resolution before arbitration, potentially rendering the arbitration agreement unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: The applicable law shall be Belgian Law, under which the dispute, controversy or claim referred to arbitration shall be decided without regard to its conflict of laws principles.
perturbation.changed_text: The applicable law shall be Belgian Law, under which the dispute, controversy or claim referred to arbitration shall be decided without regard to its conflict of laws principles. All arbitration awards are final and non-appealable.
perturbation.explanation: Adding that arbitration awards are 'non-appealable' may conflict with the right to judicial review available under the Federal Arbitration Act (FAA) in certain circumstances.
perturbation.law_explanation: While parties can agree to limit the scope of judicial review of arbitration awards, completely eliminating the right to appeal may be unenforceable under the FAA. Section 10 of the FAA allows for vacatur (setting aside) of an arbitration award in specific circumstances, such as fraud, corruption, or evident partiality of the arbitrator. A complete bar on appeals might be viewed as an impermissible waiver of these statutory rights.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 9 U.S. Code § 10 - Same; vacation; grounds; rehearing | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 9 U.S. Code § 10 - Same; vacation; grounds; rehearing U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) In any of the following cases the United States court in and for the district wherein the award was made may make an order vacating the award upon the application of any party to the arbitration— (1) where the award was procured by corruption, fraud, or undue means; (2) where there was evident partiality or corruption in the arbitrators, or either of them; (3) where the arbitrators were guilty of misconduct in refusing to postpone the hearing, upon sufficient cause shown, or in refusing to hear evidence pertinent and material to the controversy; or of any other misbehavior by which the rights of any party have been prejudiced; or (4) where the arbitrators exceeded their powers, or so imperfectly executed them that a mutual, final, and definite award upon the subject matter submitted was not made. (b) If an award is vacated and the time within which the agreement required the award to be made has not expired, the court may, in its discretion, direct a rehearing by the arbitrators. (c) The United States district court for the district wherein an award was made that was issued pursuant to section 580 of title 5 may make an order vacating the award upon the application of a person, other than a party to the arbitration, who is adversely affected or aggrieved by the award, if the use of arbitration or the award is clearly inconsistent with the factors set forth in section 572 of title 5 . (July 30, 1947, ch. 392, 61 Stat. 672 ; Pub. L. 101–552, § 5 , Nov. 15, 1990 , 104 Stat. 2745 ; Pub. L. 102–354, § 5(b)(4) , Aug. 26, 1992 , 106 Stat. 946 ; Pub. L. 107–169, § 1 , May 7, 2002 , 116 Stat. 132 .) Derivation Act Feb. 12, 1925, ch. 213, § 10, 43 Stat. 885 . Editorial Notes Amendments 2002—Subsec. (a)(1) to
perturbation.justification: The added clause stating that arbitration awards are 'non-appealable' directly contradicts the Federal Arbitration Act (FAA), specifically 9 U.S.C. § 10, which outlines grounds for vacating an arbitration award. The scraped snippets explicitly state that a U.S. court may vacate an award if it was procured by corruption, fraud, undue means, evident partiality, or misconduct by the arbitrators. By making awards 'non-appealable,' the changed text attempts to eliminate the possibility of judicial review allowed under the FAA, creating a clear legal contradiction. The FAA provides a statutory right to challenge arbitration awards under specific circumstances, which the modified clause seeks to waive entirely.
